title: Repository and working tree

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Repository and working tree; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Git Reference Documentation; current stable accessed 2026-07; section applicable to Repository and working tree
sources.claim: A Git repository stores versioned objects and references in its metadata while the working tree presents one checked-out snapshot plus local changes.

sources.claim: A Git repository stores versioned objects and references in its metadata while the working tree presents one checked-out snapshot plus local changes.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A Git repository stores versioned objects and references in its metadata while the working tree presents one checked-out snapshot plus local changes.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Editing README changes the working tree but does not alter the last commit object until new content is staged and committed. Your goal is to initialize and inspect a repository without committing generated or secret files. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Filesystem and terminal fluency; List, read and search. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A Git repository stores versioned objects and references in its metadata while the working tree presents one checked-out snapshot plus local changes. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Bare repositories have no normal working tree, nested repositories and worktrees alter layout, and untracked files are not yet represented in committed history. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: The repository is just the visible project folder, so every file in it is automatically versioned.

includes: Distinguish project files, repository metadata, tracked state and untracked files.
Initialize and inspect a repository without committing generated or secret files.
excludes: Bare repositories have no normal working tree, nested repositories and worktrees alter layout, and untracked files are not yet represented in committed history.

outcomes: Initialize and inspect a repository without committing generated or secret files.

examples.title: Repository and working tree: direct case
examples.situation: Editing README changes the working tree but does not alter the last commit object until new content is staged and committed.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: Editing README changes the working tree but does not alter the last commit object until new content is staged and committed.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Distinguish Git's object/reference database from the working tree and use status to see which visible files are tracked, modified, staged, or untracked.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Distinguish Git's object/reference database from the working tree and use status to see which visible files are tracked, modified, staged, or untracked.

examples.title: Repository and working tree: boundary case
examples.situation: Deleting the .git metadata leaves ordinary files visible but removes that directory's repository history and references.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: Deleting the .git metadata leaves ordinary files visible but removes that directory's repository history and references.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Bare repositories have no normal working tree, nested repositories and worktrees alter layout, and untracked files are not yet represented in committed history.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: The repository is just the visible project folder, so every file in it is automatically versioned.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Initialize and inspect a repository without committing generated or secret files.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: The repository is just the visible project folder, so every file in it is automatically versioned.
misconceptions.correction: Distinguish Git's object/reference database from the working tree and use status to see which visible files are tracked, modified, staged, or untracked.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: The repository is just the visible project folder, so every file in it is automatically versioned.

analogy.example: A photo studio can model Repository and working tree: the set is the working tree, a selected proof is the index, and an archived photograph is a commit snapshot.
usefulFor: Separating working changes, staged content, committed snapshots, and repository metadata
limitations: Git stores content-addressed objects and references rather than physical photographs or one linear album.
Bare repositories have no normal working tree, nested repositories and worktrees alter layout, and untracked files are not yet represented in committed history.

recap.summary: You should now be able to initialize and inspect a repository without committing generated or secret files. Keep this boundary in view: Bare repositories have no normal working tree, nested repositories and worktrees alter layout, and untracked files are not yet represented in committed history.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Repository and working tree without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: The repository is just the visible project folder, so every file in it is automatically versioned.
Apply the model to this case from memory: Deleting the .git metadata leaves ordinary files visible but removes that directory's repository history and references.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Working tree, index and HEAD”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
